label: 9_taiwan_taiwans_crossstrait_tsai
description: Discussions on the political and economic dynamics between taiwan and beijing, focusing on stability in the taiwan strait and implications for u.s. policy. These narratives highlight the evolving relationship under different leaderships and its impact on regional security.

earliest_articles.title: Trip Report: Taiwan & Hong Kong, Internal Politics and the Beijing Partnership
earliest_articles.excerpt: Taiwan Taiwan is in the middle of an effort to stabilize its relationship with China, and the process remains controversial. Prior to the presidential election of 2008, Taiwan and China were locked in a downward spiral of mutual fear, where each side feared that the other was about to challenge its fundamental…

earliest_articles.title: Taiwan Comes Between the U.S. and China Again
earliest_articles.excerpt: Not surprisingly, China is responding badly to the Pentagon’s $6-billion arms sale to Taiwan. The Beijing government has suspended security exchanges with the Pentagon and promised to sanction American defense companies. Chinese scholars and other commentators see nefarious motives in the U.S. action and warn of…

earliest_articles.title: The United States and Taiwan’s Defense Transformation
earliest_articles.excerpt: Editor’s Note: In the second installment of the Taiwan-U.S. Quarterly Analysis, former CNAPS Visiting Fellow Alexander Chieh-cheng Huang describes the long-term nature of the U.S.-ROC security relationship. Dr. Huang describes how the relationship, which has weathered strategic changes over the past 70 years and up to…

newest_articles.title: A view from Taiwan on Trump’s China visit
newest_articles.excerpt: U.S. President Donald Trump visited Beijing from May 14-15. His visit was closely watched in Taipei for signs of how Trump would approach cross-Strait relations during his summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping. Following Trump’s return to the United States, Brookings Institution Koo Chair in Taiwan Studies Ryan…

newest_articles.title: The consequences of KMT Chairwoman Cheng Li-wun’s alternative approach
newest_articles.excerpt: Kuomintang (KMT) Chairwoman Cheng Li-wun’s April 2026 visit to Beijing, which culminated in a landmark summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, has profoundly reshaped Taiwan’s political landscape and the trajectory of cross-Strait relations. newest_articles.title: How partisan is Taiwan’s security debate?
newest_articles.excerpt: In our final piece, we delve into two of the most divisive issues in Taiwan today: the special defense budget and perceptions of the United States. We examine how Taiwanese voters feel about Taiwan President Lai Ching-te’s proposed $40 billion budget and whether they believe increased defense spending strengthens…

central_articles.title: The role of diplomacy in US management of cross-Strait relations
central_articles.excerpt: This policy brief is part of a series on “Cross-Strait crossroads: Pathways for America’s Taiwan policy” produced by the Brookings Institution in partnership with RAND’s China Research Center.
